narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 65.5% chance you'll struggle with the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows strong commitment as a full-time student and identifies you as a female Asian applicant, but several academic and background factors raised concerns for the model. Specifically, your Year 1 school decile rank of 1—placing you in the bottom 10% compared to the typical student's middle ranking—had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward potential failure. Right behind that was your Year 3 decile rank, also a 1, which similarly signaled lower performance relative to peers and reinforced the risk. Your LSAT score of 24, well below the average of about 37, was the next biggest factor, as lower scores like yours often correlate with bar challenges in the data. Family income in the low bracket, compared to the dataset's higher average, added to this concern, as did your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 versus the typical 3.2. Your racial background as Asian, underrepresented at just 6% against 84% White students, and gender played smaller roles but still nudged the outlook unfavorably in this context. Overall, these features ranked in influence as: early-year grades first, later-year grades second, LSAT third, family income fourth, race fifth, and undergrad GPA sixth, with enrollment status and gender having minimal impact. Compared to typical admitted students, your metrics across grades, test scores, and GPA are notably lower, tipping the model toward caution. The good news is several of these are changeable: retaking the LSAT for a higher score could dramatically shift future predictions, as it's a top influencer. Focusing on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or grad school performance would address the leading factors head-on. Building a higher undergrad-equivalent record, perhaps via post-bac programs, and exploring financial aid options might also help offset income-related signals over time. With targeted prep like bar-focused tutoring or study habits tailored to your strengths, many students in similar spots have turned predictions around—keep pursuing your passion, and consider reapplying after these improvements.
